nodes.label: Personal Training Foundations manual (converted source B, chapter-structured)
Chapter 12: Working with Injuries — The MEAT Protocol
Chapter 13: Client Management and Coaching Philosophy
Appendix: Health Assessments and Recommended Resources
Stacking (neutral spine)
nodes.rationale: Ribs parallel to pelvis allows lifting the heaviest weight with least injury chance; load travels straight down through vertebrae rather than creating shearing force.

nodes.label: Bracing (intra-abdominal pressure / Valsalva Manoeuvre)
nodes.rationale: Filling core with air and contracting to produce a pressurised cylinder of spinal support; forceful expiration against closed glottis creates high intra-abdominal pressure, turning the torso into a pressurised structure like a sealed can.

nodes.label: Zercher position / Cable & Barbell Zercher Squat
nodes.rationale: Used as the primary teaching tool for stacking and bracing because the compressive load automatically elicits a brace, giving the client a physical reference point rather than an abstract verbal cue.

nodes.label: Goblet Squat
nodes.rationale: Weight held at chest acts as a counterbalance pulling torso into upright stacked position — first loaded squat variation typically given to new clients; elbows-inside-knees is a visible safety checkpoint.

nodes.label: The Hinge (hip hinge movement pattern)
nodes.rationale: Pushing hips back loads the largest muscles (hamstrings, glutes, erectors); taught by analogy to picking up a heavy suitcase versus sitting into a chair (squat).

nodes.label: External vs. Internal Coaching Cues
nodes.rationale: Research consistently shows external points of focus (outcome in environment) produce faster skill acquisition and superior movement quality than internal cues (specific muscles/joints), because it lets the nervous system self-organise the many small muscle actions rather than requiring conscious micromanagement.

nodes.label: Romanian Deadlift (RDL)
nodes.rationale: Weight should travel no lower than mid-shin height — it is about how far back the hips push, not how low the weight goes.

nodes.label: Breathing into the lower ribs (360-degree breathing)
nodes.rationale: Expanding ribcage anterior, posterior, and lateral creates the greatest possible spinal support; most untrained people breathe shallowly via chest/shoulders which does little to support the spine.

nodes.label: Weightlifting Belt usage guidance
nodes.rationale: Not needed until lifting moderately heavy loads; teach the brace first, add belt only once brace is reliable — belt should not become a crutch masking a weak brace.

nodes.label: Australian Pull-Ups (Inverted Row)
nodes.rationale: Scalable — can be adjusted from near-upright (deconditioned client) to near-parallel (stronger client), letting one piece of equipment serve a mixed-ability group class.

nodes.label: Dumbbell Rows (Shallow Lat / One-Arm / Supported Two-Arm)
Split Squats
nodes.rationale: Unilateral training exposes and corrects side-to-side strength imbalances a bilateral squat can hide; loads the spine less than a loaded bilateral squat for the same leg effort.

nodes.label: Free Weights vs. Machines vs. Cables
nodes.rationale: No single tool is superior — free weights for main compound lifts requiring full-body stabilisation, machines to push near failure safely, cables for angled isolation work.

nodes.label: Deadlift (Conventional)
nodes.rationale: Combination of a squat and a Romanian Deadlift — begins like an RDL but weight is lowered fully to the floor and picked back up.

nodes.label: Nordic Curls
nodes.rationale: Builds hamstrings' ability to control the knee while lengthened — directly protective against sprint/change-of-direction hamstring strains.

nodes.label: Plank
nodes.rationale: Coach for quality of stacked position first; duration follows naturally as quality improves — holding a sagging position longer is worse than a short, well-stacked hold.

nodes.label: Energy Balance — Calories In vs. Calories Out (CICO)
nodes.rationale: The body stores excess consumed calories as fat; understanding CICO is the foundation of any diet. Track meals for 7 days via apps like MyFitnessPal/HealthifyMe to find maintenance intake.

nodes.label: How to Create the Perfect Diet (protein anchor, veg every meal, smart carbs)
nodes.rationale: Rather than rigid meal plans, three principles (anchor plate with protein, vegetables every meal, choose carbs wisely) form a simple repeatable framework scaling to any goal/preference/cultural diet.

nodes.label: Smart Supplementation (Multivitamin, Creatine, Protein Powder, etc.)
nodes.rationale: Most supplements are untested or harmful; a quality multivitamin and creatine monohydrate offer the best evidence-to-cost ratio, everything else is situational.

nodes.label: Managing Cravings
nodes.rationale: Reduce the trigger itself rather than relying on willpower alone — avoid high-sugar processed foods that spark further cravings.

nodes.label: Tips to Strengthen Digestion
nodes.rationale: Digestion is framed as the single most important thing to fix, with a profound cascading effect on everything else.

nodes.label: Flexibility vs. Mobility
nodes.rationale: Flexibility = passive range via gravity/implements; Mobility = active range via muscular force. Rather than dedicated stretching, train with full range of motion in exercises.

nodes.label: Warm-Up Philosophy (exercise itself at lower weight)
Exercise Order (heavy/fast first, light/high-rep later)
nodes.rationale: Fatigue from lower-intensity work impairs subsequent explosive performance more than the reverse ordering — analogized to playing basketball before a 2-hour jog, not after.

nodes.label: Sets, Repetitions and Rest guidance
nodes.rationale: Lower reps (heavier) need more sets/rest; higher reps need fewer sets/less rest, though high-rep sets near failure may still need 3-4 min rest regardless of load.

nodes.label: DOMS (Delayed Onset Muscle Soreness) fallacy
nodes.rationale: Soreness by itself does not indicate workout effectiveness — don't chase soreness, chase progressive overload.

nodes.label: Cardio: Long Cardio vs. Interval Cardio
Walking as the most powerful lifestyle recommendation
nodes.rationale: Not Zone 2 cardio for most people but shares long-term health benefits; accessibility, near-zero recovery cost, and no skill requirement make it the single most powerful lifestyle recommendation a coach can make.

nodes.label: Bro Split (Body Part training split)
nodes.rationale: Each muscle group trained once weekly — allows volume without carryover soreness, but a missed session costs a full week for that body part; suits reliable 5-day/week trainees.

nodes.label: Upper/Lower training split
nodes.rationale: Trains each muscle group twice weekly instead of once — research favours this over bro split at equal weekly volume; a missed session only costs one upper or lower day.

nodes.label: Push-Pull-Legs training split
nodes.rationale: Groups by movement pattern; 6-day repeating cycle gives each muscle group two full training days weekly, similar to upper/lower but more exercises per body part per session.

nodes.label: Full Body Workouts (FBW) — Recommended Default
nodes.rationale: Recommended default for general population — more days practising key lifts, missing a session never leaves a muscle group untrained for the whole week; consistency beats the perfect split.

nodes.label: Progressive Overload
nodes.rationale: The most important principle for long-term progress — each session must exceed the body's prior adaptation, via weight, reps, technique quality, or reduced rest.

nodes.label: Training Across Populations (athletes, sex, youth, older adults)
nodes.rationale: Core principles (stacking, bracing, external cues, progressive overload) apply to every client; what changes between populations is how much intensity/volume/novelty the client's body and life circumstances can absorb.

nodes.label: RICE Protocol critique
nodes.rationale: Letting an injury settle through passivity; ice impedes blood flow. Pain does not always equal injury — disc bulges present in 19% of asymptomatic people over 40, illustrating pain's complexity via nervous system sensitisation.

nodes.label: MEAT Protocol (Movement, Exercise, Analgesia, Treatment)
nodes.rationale: More effective acronym replacing RICE for injury management — Movement (non-provoking ranges early), Exercise (progressive conventional loading once pain subsides), Analgesia (natural healing aids), Treatment (manual therapies, physical modalities).

nodes.label: Prehabilitation
nodes.rationale: Strengthening joints/tissues before accidental injury occurs — e.g. addressing shoulder instability with light overhead work before it becomes an injury.

nodes.label: No single "correct" exercise form — strength is range-specific
nodes.rationale: A stacked spine is the strongest position but not the only safe one. Training only in the neutral range makes you strong only in that range; goal is strength/resilience across all ranges over time.

nodes.label: Exercises for Lengthened Positions (Poliquin Split Squat, Jefferson Curls, etc.)
nodes.rationale: Muscles trained only in shortened position stay weak/vulnerable at the lengthened/stretched range — precisely where most non-contact strains occur. Requires slower, more thorough warm-up.

nodes.label: Exercises for Shortened Positions (Knee Extensions, Back Extensions, etc.)
Paul Chek's 1-2-3-4 System
nodes.rationale: Reminds coaches that bodies do not exist in a vacuum — embedded within a life, purpose, and daily choices that build or deplete health.

nodes.label: 3 — Choices (Optimal/Suboptimal/No Choice)
nodes.rationale: A middle option (e.g. shorter gentler session) converts an all-or-nothing decision into one the client can actually say yes to.

nodes.label: 4 — The Four Doctors (Diet, Quiet, Movement, Happiness)
Nasal vs. Mouth Breathing
nodes.rationale: Nasal breathing preferred: heats cold air, catches particles, aids nitric oxide absorption, keeps oxygen levels stable. Mouth breathing is a fair compromise only at high intensity (inhale nose, exhale mouth).

nodes.label: Visualisation (mental rehearsal)
nodes.rationale: Sports science shows mental rehearsal produces measurable improvements in physical output even without additional physical practice.

nodes.label: Adapt to the Client — Not the Other Way Around
nodes.rationale: Strict-guru coaching fails more often than it succeeds with general population clients; kind, gradual, non-confrontational suggestions produce more compliance than blunt demands.

nodes.label: Professional Boundaries (basis of the coaching relationship)
nodes.rationale: Coach is paid in money and respect; if a client repeatedly disrespects time/payment/communication and behavior doesn't change after conversations, release the client.

nodes.label: Make Fitness Their Best Friend
nodes.rationale: Prefer exercise approaches clients enjoy over strictly optimal ones for general population; celebrate performance milestones over slow-changing appearance metrics.

nodes.label: Resolve Issues Quickly
nodes.rationale: Every issue should be acknowledged instantly even if not solved instantly — dramatically increases perceived coach value.

nodes.label: Optimal Theory of Learning (Autonomy, Enhanced Expectations, External Focus)
nodes.rationale: Three factors that most powerfully accelerate skill acquisition: autonomy (freedom of choice increases adherence), enhanced expectations (sincere belief in capacity generates capacity), external focus (faster automatic skill learning than internal body-part focus).

nodes.label: Personal Training (1-on-1 mode)
nodes.rationale: Advantages: individual assessment/fine-tuning, strong coach-client bond, lowest injury chance. Disadvantages: training must be made more engaging alone, no peer comparison, requires strong communication skill from coach.

nodes.label: Group Training mode
nodes.rationale: Advantages: competitive effort, accountability, high referral potential from volume. Disadvantages: no individual assessment, higher injury rates, limited per-client payment unless multiple large batches.

nodes.label: PT vs Group Training — hybrid business model
nodes.rationale: A sustainable common model combines a small 1-on-1 client roster for steady higher per-session income and deep relationships with one or two group classes weekly for community, referrals, and training more people per time block.

nodes.label: 3 Health Assessments (Heart Rate, Sleep Quality, Breath Hold)
Recommended Resources (Certifications, Key Texts, Online Resources)
Science and Practice of Strength Training (Vladimir Zatsiorsky)
How to Eat, Move and Be Healthy (Paul Chek)
Dr. Greg Lehman ("calm shit down and build shit up")

hyperedges.label: Zercher-position teaching flow for stacking and bracing
Core coaching-philosophy pillars (adapt to client, boundaries, fitness enjoyment, quick resolution, optimal learning theory)
